# Transcrição: 04-ACTIVITY- Mapping Emotions for Your Personal Story

- **Fonte:** 04-ACTIVITY- Mapping Emotions for Your Personal Story
- **Duração:** 02:03
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 02:03]

All right, I want you to do the same thing now for your personal story. I want you to lay it out and I want you to map out those feelings. There's a couple ways you can do this, so I want to go into a little detail for you. First of all, we have to acknowledge that sometimes we feel more than one thing. That's really challenging and storytelling, but it is true. My suggestion is if you run into a scene where you were feeling two things, I was happy but frightened. I was sad, but hopeful. You're going to map out both of those, which is to say you're going to include both of those emotions, but you're probably going to identify the one that is stronger and lean into that one more. It's hard to argue two things, so you probably have to choose the one that makes the most sense to you. If you're a visual person, I've worked with lots of people who actually highlight the outline of their comic book pain or shade their comic book pain with the emotion that they felt in color. A sad moment gets blue, an angry moment gets red, a peaceful moment gets purple or pink. That also can be helpful in terms of remembering your story. If you're one of those people who sees everything and remembers everything they see, you may want to use some colors to map out those emotional moments. Also, don't limit yourself to one word. Oftentimes our emotions are very complex. We're not looking for brevity when we're trying to identify our emotional map. We're looking for accuracy, specificity. If you are not frightened, but you are anxious in a way that you know you shouldn't be and yet you are but wish you weren't, write that down. That is a very complex, interesting, and frankly fascinating way to feel. If you can get your audience to get that sense of anxiety that you know is not justified and yet it is present but you wish it wasn't, we all understand that feeling. Use as many words as you need to create that emotional map, identifying each one individually with as much specificity as possible. Go ahead and do that now.
